steps.text: Determine whether the scope of experiments conducted at the facility were consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.
Determine whether the licensee’s procedures for the control and conduct of experiments are consistent with regulatory requirements and licensee commitments.

Determine whether the licensee reviewed and approved experiments performed at the facility, and any subsequent changes made to the experiments, in accordance with Technical Specification (TS) requirements and the licensee’s procedural requirements.
Determine whether the licensee reviewed experiments for TS changes or compliance to 10 CFR 50.59.

Determine whether the licensee identified hazards associated with the experiments conducted since the last inspection and took appropriate remedial action, in accordance with the licensee’s procedures.
Determine whether the licensee predicted the approximate reactivity effect on the reactor before conducting the experiment.
Determine whether the licensee confirmed the reactivity effect as required by the TS.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.

Determine whether the licensee accounted for irradiated items until disposing of them or until they decayed to an acceptable level, in accordance with regulatory requirements and applicable licensee procedures.
Determine whether the licensee’s experiments are constrained as required by the TS.
Determine whether researchers adhered to the licensees procedural controls when loading experiments into the test facilities and removing them from the test facilities.
Determine whether the experiments reduced the operator’s ability to control reactivity manipulations or the protective system’s ability to shut down the reactor if needed.
Determine whether engineered radiation safety controls were used during experiments and posting was accomplished as required.
Examine new experiments and changes to the licensee’s experiments and related programs since the last inspection.

preconditions.text: The facilities and materials must be licensed under 10 CFR Part 50.
The licensee must have committed in writing to use the specific guidance document for it to be used as requirements.

constraints.text: Do not use this procedure for the inspection of facilities and materials governed by other types of licenses.
Naturally occurring radioactive materials or those produced by an accelerator are not licensed by the NRC.

steps.text: Review 50 percent of the new experiments or changes to experiments since the last inspection.
Compare the list of experiments to respective license conditions, TS, Safety Analysis Report (SAR), Safety Evaluation Report (SER), and supporting documentation.
Review available documentation at the office prior to the onsite inspection.
Review the licensee's procedures for the control and conduct of experiments prior to the onsite inspection.
Directly observe 50 percent of the new experiments, or changes to experiments, since the last inspection.
Review records and discuss with cognizant personnel.

preconditions.text: Experiments or changes have not been previously reviewed by NRC personnel
Documentation is already available at the office
It is practical to review procedures prior to the onsite inspection

constraints.text: Procedures for experiments must be governed by the TS, license conditions, and the licensee's administrative controls.
Specific procedures must be developed for each non-routine experiment.

steps.text: Review records and conduct discussions with cognizant personnel.
Review the information available to the operator that authorizes the conduct of a particular experiment.

constraints.text: Particular attention is to be given to experiments that contain special nuclear material (fueled experiments) or explosives.

steps.text: Document routine experiments individually for each new group of experimenters, or whenever the experiment has not been performed for one calendar year or more.
Approve routine experiments by the reactor supervisor, an SRO, or the radiation control officer.
Obtain safety review committee approval for experiments that could pose a hazard to the safety of the reactor, personnel, or the public.
Obtain approval from the reactor supervisor, an SRO (if the reactor supervisor is not an SRO), and the radiation control officer for experiments that could pose a hazard.
Verify that all expressed conditions are met before the experiment is conducted.
Obtain safety review committee approval for experiments that have a significant potential for posing a hazard.
Obtain approval from the reactor supervisor, an SRO (if the reactor supervisor is not an SRO), and the radiation control officer for experiments with significant hazard potential.
Insert, disassemble, or remove the experiment from the reactor.

preconditions.text: A written description of reactor operations (from insertion until removal) approved by the safety review committee is required before an experiment is inserted in the reactor.

constraints.text: Routine experiments must pose no hazard to the reactor, the personnel, or the public.
Significant hazard experiments must be inserted, disassembled, or removed under the direct supervision of the reactor supervisor, or a duly authorized SRO.

steps.text: List items such as pressure and vacuum gauges in experiment proposals before the experiment is begun.
Plan remedial actions in accordance with the licensee's administrative control procedures.
Conduct experiments in accordance with the licensee's administrative control procedures.
Monitor and understand the meaning of gauges that measure pressures, including vacuum conditions, where appropriate to the experimental setup.

preconditions.text: Before an experiment is inserted in the reactor

constraints.text: The use of explosives within a reactor facility requires NRC review and approval.
If the capsule has ruptured, the opening of the experiment tube could be radiologically hazardous or cause a reactivity transient.
Operators and experimenters must understand the hazards associated with an experiment and the methods to identify and respond to them.

steps.text: Verify that the applicable TS limits for reactivity, such as shutdown margin, excess reactivity, and individual and total worth of experiments were not exceeded.
Verify the location and the status of three past experiments, performed under the license since the last inspection.

preconditions.text: If the regulating rod does not change position substantially with the experiment insertion

constraints.text: Until final disposal, irradiated articles are to be controlled by the licensee in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20.
Final disposal shall be made in accordance with Subpart K of 10 CFR Part 20.

steps.text: Perform final disposal of irradiated articles in accordance with Subpart K of 10 CFR Part 20.
Verify that the recipient is licensed to possess the type, form, and quantity of material to be transferred.
Transfer byproduct material in accordance with 10 CFR 30.41.
Establish secure arrangements so that a single movement does not pulse a non-pulsing reactor, or exceed TS requirements or safety analysis assumptions.

constraints.text: Irradiated articles must be controlled by the licensee in accordance with 10 CFR Part 20 until final disposal.
The TS require various degrees of constraint for experiments depending on reactivity values.
Inadequate physical constraints in a pool-type reactor can cause an experiment to float to the surface, causing potential reactivity and exposure problems.

steps.text: Discuss the methods of handling experiments and examine the process for consistency with the licensee’s administrative controls and procedures.

preconditions.text: Removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.

constraints.text: An experiment in a pool-type reactor must be adequately constrained to prevent it from escaping constraints and floating to the surface, causing potential reactivity and exposure problems.
If the experiment has a reactivity worth of between 0.002 and around 0.006 delta K/K, it must be mildly constrained (e.g., resting on a support instead of dangling from a string or wire).
Experiments with a worth of less than 0.002 delta K/K can be inserted or removed while critical only if the expected radiation field for personnel does not present an exposure problem.
If the worth of the experiment is greater than 0.006 delta K/K, it must be fixed in place such that a simple movement cannot change its position.
Experiments of negative reactivity on insertion must meet TS requirements upon withdrawal (as they are positive on withdrawal).
Experimental activities are required to be consistent with the licensee’s approved administrative controls and procedures for experiments.
When an experiment is being removed from the reactor, it is assumed to be contaminated and highly radioactive until proven otherwise.

steps.text: Discuss the methods of handling experiments if removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.
Examine the process for consistency with the licensee’s administrative controls and procedures.

Determine how the licensee assures that experiments do not cause reactivity anomalies that cannot be controlled by the operator or control system, considering operator's control over rabbit insertions/removals, detector shadowing, and control rod/blade interference.
Verify if the licensee has considered the use of engineered safety features (such as remote handling devices, time, distance, and shielding) in the planning and conduct of experiments.

preconditions.text: Removal of an experiment from the reactor cannot be directly observed.

constraints.text: Access to areas where radiation is present must be posted in accordance with 10 CFR Parts 20.1902 and 20.1903 and limited as required by the licensee's radiation protection program.
